title: Furcaria cornuta
taxonomy: Furcaria cornuta is a species of liverwort, a non-vascular land plant that belongs to the class Marchantiopsida. This classification places it within the division Marchantiophyta, which includes all liverworts. The class Marchantiopsida is further divided into several orders, with Furcaria cornuta being part of the order Marchantiales. Within the family Marchantiaceae, Furcaria cornuta is specifically classified as a member of the genus Furcaria. Its classification is essential for understanding its unique characteristics and evolutionary relationships.

intro: Furcaria cornuta, also known as Irish moss or red algae, is a type of edible red algae that belongs to the family Furcellariaceae. It has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can help to soothe digestive issues, reduce inflammation, and boost the immune system. The bioactive constituents of Furcaria cornuta include polysaccharides, particularly carrageenan, which is a sulfated polysaccharide with potential therapeutic applications, as well as other compounds like fucoidan and furcellaran. Furcaria cornuta is commonly used in herbal preparations such as capsules, powders, and teas, often in combination with other herbs, to support digestive health and immune function.
